title: Databases — Where Data Lives
subtitle: SQL, tables, joins, transactions, and when to use NoSQL

sections.title: Tables, Rows & Relationships
sections.text:
Relational databases store data in tables — like spreadsheets with rows and columns. A users table might have columns for id, name, email, and created_at. Each row represents one user.

Tables are connected through foreign keys. An orders table references user_id to link each order to a user. This creates relationships: one user has many orders (one-to-many).

Indexes speed up lookups. Without an index, finding a user by email requires scanning every row. With an index on the email column, the database uses a B-tree structure to find the row in O(log n) time.

config.caption: users and orders tables connected by a foreign key — one user has many orders

sections.caption: Tables connected by foreign keys form the backbone of relational databases

sections.title: Joins & Transactions
sections.text:
Joins let you query across related tables in a single statement. An INNER JOIN returns only rows with matching keys in both tables. A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, filling nulls where no match exists on the right.

Transactions group multiple operations into a single atomic unit. Either all operations succeed (commit) or none do (rollback). This is critical for operations like transferring money between accounts — if the debit succeeds but the credit fails, the transaction rolls back both.

items.heading: INNER JOIN
items.body: Rows that match in both tables. Most common join type.

items.heading: LEFT JOIN
items.body: All rows from left table + matching right rows. Nulls for no match.

items.heading: ACID Transactions
items.body: Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable — the four guarantees.

items.heading: N+1 Problem
items.body: Querying N rows + 1 query per row = N+1 queries. Use eager loading instead.

sections.caption: Key SQL concepts: joins combine tables, transactions ensure data integrity
sections.callout: Always use transactions for operations that must succeed or fail together. Money transfers, order creation, and account registration all need atomicity.

sections.title: SQL vs NoSQL
sections.text:
SQL databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite) use structured schemas, enforce relationships with foreign keys, and guarantee ACID compliance. They excel at complex queries, joins, and data integrity.

NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra) trade ACID guarantees for flexibility and scale. MongoDB stores documents (JSON-like), Redis is a key-value store, Cassandra uses wide columns. NoSQL is great for rapid prototyping, hierarchical data, and horizontal scaling.

items: Best for: real-time, IoT, content management

sections.caption: SQL for consistency and relationships, NoSQL for flexibility and scale
sections.detail: You don't have to choose one. Many production systems use both — PostgreSQL for transactional data and Redis for caching, or MongoDB for content and Kafka for event streams. This is called polyglot persistence. The right tool depends on your access patterns, consistency requirements, and scale needs.
